summary: Deep tendon reflexes remain preserved in most instances of myasthenia gravis; thus, their absence would indicate an exception among listed options. Key here lies understanding how specific neuromuscular symptoms correlate distinctly within diagnostic parameters of Myasthenia Gravis versus other potential causes affecting these same pathways indirectly through different mechanisms like those involving direct LMN damage leading to hypo/hypereflexias not characteristic of primary MG pathology.
